architecture.summary: FisherSketch is a training-free source-selection method that estimates head Fisher alignment in LLM families with shared vocabularies by computing a cosine similarity between kernel mean embeddings in the joint activation-error space. Rather than materializing prohibitively large Fisher matrices, the method uses a single streaming pass over task data to accumulate activation and error statistics (m=4096 dimensions), encoding transfer-relevant update structure in compact task signatures (16 KB) and per-task streaming state (192 KB) suitable for storage alongside model hashes. The system decomposes transfer similarity into activation, error, and coupling factors without requiring explicit Fisher matrix computation.

biblio.title: The Geometry of Updates: Fisher Alignment at Vocabulary Scale

claims.statement: Head Fisher alignment is exactly a cosine between kernel mean embeddings in the joint activation-error space
Representation similarity metrics (e.g., CKA) are non-identifiable for transfer; models can share identical representations yet have orthogonal head updates
FisherSketch estimates head Fisher alignment in a single streaming pass with K=128,256 alignment comparisons, using a 16 KB task signature (m=4096) and 192 KB per-task streaming state
FisherSketch remains informative when activation similarity cannot distinguish tasks, as demonstrated by shared-parameter and internal-layer validations on Llama-3.1-8B verbalizer-shift experiments
FisherSketch provides a diagnostic instrument for studying whether LLM task similarity is driven by activations, errors, or their coupling
